text: The curve C might be parametrized by a variable s ( s might be the arc length, for example) as follows: \( \quad x=x(s), y=y(s) \) \[ \overrightarrow{d s}=d x \vec{i}+d y \vec{j}=\left[\frac{d x}{d s} \vec{i}+\frac{d y}{d s} \vec{j}\right] d s \] \( \overrightarrow{d s} \quad \) is the line element along the curve C . The line integral can then be written as \[ \int_{a}^{b} \vec{A} \cdot \overrightarrow{d s}=\int_{a}^{b}\left(A_{1} \vec{i}+A_{2} \vec{j}\right) \cdot\left[\frac{d x}{d s} \vec{i}+\frac{d y}{d s} \vec{j}\right] d s=\int_{a}^{b}\left(A_{1} \frac{d x}{d s}+A_{2} \frac{d y}{d s}\right) d s \] If the curve C is closed we denote the line integral along C as \( \oint \vec{A} \cdot \overrightarrow{d s} \) It is also called contour integral. 2-10
raw_text: The curve C might be parametrized by a variable s ( s might be the arc length, for example) as follows: \( \quad x=x(s), y=y(s) \) \[ \overrightarrow{d s}=d x \vec{i}+d y \vec{j}=\left[\frac{d x}{d s} \vec{i}+\frac{d y}{d s} \vec{j}\right] d s \] \( \overrightarrow{d s} \quad \) is the line element along the curve C . The line integral can then be written as \[ \int_{a}^{b} \vec{A} \cdot \overrightarrow{d s}=\int_{a}^{b}\left(A_{1} \vec{i}+A_{2} \vec{j}\right) \cdot\left[\frac{d x}{d s} \vec{i}+\frac{d y}{d s} \vec{j}\right] d s=\int_{a}^{b}\left(A_{1} \frac{d x}{d s}+A_{2} \frac{d y}{d s}\right) d s \] If the curve C is closed we denote the line integral along C as \( \oint \vec{A} \cdot \overrightarrow{d s} \) It is also called contour integral. 2-10

text: Example: Potential or free Vortex \[ \vec{V}=\frac{\left\langle U_{r}, U_{\theta}, U_{z}\right\rangle}{\text { cylindrical }}=\left\langle 0,-\frac{\Gamma}{2 \pi r}, 0\right\rangle \] What is \( \oint_{c} \vec{u} \cdot d \vec{s} \) for a gener \( r>0 \) ? \[ \begin{aligned} \oint_{c} \vec{u} \cdot d \vec{s} & =\int_{0}^{2 \pi}\left(-\frac{\Gamma}{2 \pi x} \hat{e}_{\theta}\right) \cdot\left(\hat{r} d \theta \hat{e}_{\theta}\right) \\ & =\int_{0}^{2 \pi}\left(-\frac{\Gamma}{2 \pi}\right) \underbrace{r}_{d s=r d \theta}(\underbrace{r}_{1} \cdot \hat{e}_{\theta}) d \theta=-\frac{\Gamma}{2 \pi} \cdot 2 \pi=-\Gamma \\ & =\text { Circulation or Vortex Strength } \end{aligned} \] 2-11
raw_text: Example: Potential or free Vortex \[ \vec{V}=\frac{\left\langle U_{r}, U_{\theta}, U_{z}\right\rangle}{\text { cylindrical }}=\left\langle 0,-\frac{\Gamma}{2 \pi r}, 0\right\rangle \] What is \( \oint_{c} \vec{u} \cdot d \vec{s} \) for a gener \( r>0 \) ? \[ \begin{aligned} \oint_{c} \vec{u} \cdot d \vec{s} & =\int_{0}^{2 \pi}\left(-\frac{\Gamma}{2 \pi x} \hat{e}_{\theta}\right) \cdot\left(\hat{r} d \theta \hat{e}_{\theta}\right) \\ & =\int_{0}^{2 \pi}\left(-\frac{\Gamma}{2 \pi}\right) \underbrace{r}_{d s=r d \theta}(\underbrace{r}_{1} \cdot \hat{e}_{\theta}) d \theta=-\frac{\Gamma}{2 \pi} \cdot 2 \pi=-\Gamma \\ & =\text { Circulation or Vortex Strength } \end{aligned} \] 2-11

text: Surface Integrals Consider a surface \( S \) bounded by curve \( C \) \( d S \) is a differential surface element \( \hat{n} \) is a unit vector normal to \( d S \). In fluid mechanics we often need to integrate quantities over a surface where the quantity depends on the surface orientation. 2-12
raw_text: Surface Integrals Consider a surface \( S \) bounded by curve \( C \) \( d S \) is a differential surface element \( \hat{n} \) is a unit vector normal to \( d S \). In fluid mechanics we often need to integrate quantities over a surface where the quantity depends on the surface orientation. 2-12

text: For example, since pressure acts normal to the surface at any point, the total force due to pressure acting on a surface of a body is equal to \[ * \vec{F}=\downarrow \iint_{S} p \vec{n} d S \] The combination \( \vec{n} d S \) is also written as \( d \vec{S} \). We will also have surface integrals of the form: \[ \begin{array}{l} \iint_{S} \vec{A} \cdot \vec{n} d S=\iint_{S} \underline{\vec{A} \cdot \overrightarrow{d S}} \leftarrow \text { Scalar Value! } \\ \iint_{S} \vec{A} \times \vec{n} d S=\iint_{S} \vec{A} \times \overrightarrow{d S} \longleftarrow \text { Vector! } \end{array} \] 2-13
raw_text: For example, since pressure acts normal to the surface at any point, the total force due to pressure acting on a surface of a body is equal to \[ * \vec{F}=\downarrow \iint_{S} p \vec{n} d S \] The combination \( \vec{n} d S \) is also written as \( d \vec{S} \). We will also have surface integrals of the form: \[ \begin{array}{l} \iint_{S} \vec{A} \cdot \vec{n} d S=\iint_{S} \underline{\vec{A} \cdot \overrightarrow{d S}} \leftarrow \text { Scalar Value! } \\ \iint_{S} \vec{A} \times \vec{n} d S=\iint_{S} \vec{A} \times \overrightarrow{d S} \longleftarrow \text { Vector! } \end{array} \] 2-13

text: If the surface is closed (so that it encloses a volume) then is the outward normal and the integrals are denoted \[ \begin{array}{l} \text { doestes } \\ \text { cosses surface. } \end{array} \oiint_{S} p \vec{n} d S, \quad \oiint_{S} \vec{A} \cdot \vec{n} d S, \quad \oiint_{S} \vec{A} \times \vec{n} d S \] Surface integrals - what is \( d S \) ? 1. Rectangular area \[ \begin{array}{l} \hat{n}=\hat{k} \\ d s=d x d y \end{array} \] 2. Circular area \[ \begin{array}{l} \hat{n}=\hat{k} \text { or } \hat{e}_{z} \\ d S=r d r d \theta \end{array} \] \[ \int_{0}^{2 \pi} \int_{0}^{R} f(\cdots) r d r d \theta \] don't funct this or I set sad. 3. Outer surface of a circular cylinder \[ \begin{array}{l} \hat{n}=\hat{e}^{r} \\ d s=\hat{R} d \theta d z \end{array} \] \[ \int_{0}^{H} \int_{0}^{2 \pi} f(\cdots) R d \theta d z \] 4. Surface of a sphere Redius of Calimar is a constant! 2-14 \[ \int_{0}^{2 \pi} \int_{0}^{\pi} f(\ldots) R^{2} \sin \theta d \theta d \phi \] ard \( \hat{n}=\hat{e}_{r} \) End 9/15/25

text: Volume Integrals - If we integrate a quantity over a closed volume V it is written as \( \oiiint A d V \quad \) if A is a scalar quantity \( \oiiint_{V} \vec{A}^{V} d V \) if \( \vec{A} \) is a vector quantity. For example, Mass of fluid in volume \( V=\oiiint \rho d V \) \[ \text { Mass }=\int_{0}^{L_{z}} \int_{0}^{L_{y}} \int_{0}^{L_{x}} p(x, y, z) \underbrace{d x d y d z}_{\Delta V} \] \[ \text { Mass }=\int_{0}^{A} \int_{0}^{R} \int_{0}^{2 \pi} p(r, \theta, z) \underbrace{r d \theta d r d z}_{d V} \] Remember: Cylindrical Remember: Culindricel condirate Churled courdinate chumld "er" hargin aroun!
raw_text: Volume Integrals - If we integrate a quantity over a closed volume V it is written as \( \oiiint A d V \quad \) if A is a scalar quantity \( \oiiint_{V} \vec{A}^{V} d V \) if \( \vec{A} \) is a vector quantity. For example, Mass of fluid in volume \( V=\oiiint \rho d V \) \[ \text { Mass }=\int_{0}^{L_{z}} \int_{0}^{L_{y}} \int_{0}^{L_{x}} p(x, y, z) \underbrace{d x d y d z}_{\Delta V} \] \[ \text { Mass }=\int_{0}^{A} \int_{0}^{R} \int_{0}^{2 \pi} p(r, \theta, z) \underbrace{r d \theta d r d z}_{d V} \] Remember: Cylindrical Remember: Culindricel condirate Churled courdinate chumld "er" hargin aroun!

abla \cdot \vec{f}) d V \]

text: Control Volumes 1. Material volume - consists of the same fluid elements at all 2. Fixed control volume - fixed volume in space through *(We will derive the equations of motion using fixed control 2-17 volumes)

text: Volumetric flow rate \( d \dot{Q} \) = rate of flow of volume of fluid through a differential surface \( d S \). \[ [d \dot{Q}]=\frac{\mathrm{m}^{3}}{\mathrm{~s}}=\vec{u} \cdot d \vec{s}=\vec{u} \cdot \vec{n} d S \] \( d \mid \dot{Q} d t \) = volume of fluid flowing through a differential surface \( d S \) in a time \( d t \). \[ d \dot{Q} d t=\vec{u} \cdot \vec{n} d t d S \] \[ d \dot{Q}=\vec{u} \cdot \vec{n} d S= \] 2-18
raw_text: Volumetric flow rate \( d \dot{Q} \) = rate of flow of volume of fluid through a differential surface \( d S \). \[ [d \dot{Q}]=\frac{\mathrm{m}^{3}}{\mathrm{~s}}=\vec{u} \cdot d \vec{s}=\vec{u} \cdot \vec{n} d S \] \( d \mid \dot{Q} d t \) = volume of fluid flowing through a differential surface \( d S \) in a time \( d t \). \[ d \dot{Q} d t=\vec{u} \cdot \vec{n} d t d S \] \[ d \dot{Q}=\vec{u} \cdot \vec{n} d S= \] 2-18

text: \( \dot{Q}= \) volumetric flow rate of fluid through a surface \( S \). \[ \dot{Q}=\iint_{S} d \dot{Q}=\iint_{S * \dot{Q}=U \cdot A} \vec{U} \cdot \vec{n} d S \quad \begin{array}{l} \forall \cdot L \\ \rightarrow(U, V, W) \text { is uniform and to } A! \end{array} \text { All Scomeon . } \] Example. Velocity field is \( \vec{u}=(3,4,0) \mathrm{m} / \mathrm{s} \). Calculate volumetric flow rates through \( 1 \mathrm{~m}^{2} \) surface \( S \) if a) \( \vec{n}=(1,0,0) \) b) \( \vec{n}=(0,1,0) \) c) \( \vec{n}=\left(\frac{3}{5}, \frac{4}{5}, 0\right) \) d) \( \vec{n}=\left(\frac{4}{5},-\frac{3}{5}, 0\right) \) a.) \( \dot{Q}=\iint_{S}(\vec{u} \cdot \vec{n}) d S=\iint_{S}\langle 3,4,0\rangle \cdot\langle 1,0,0\rangle d S=\iint_{S} 3 d S=3 \iint_{S} d S=3 \mathrm{~m}^{2} / \mathrm{s} \) b.) \( \dot{Q}=\iint\langle 3,4,0\rangle \cdot\langle 0,1,0\rangle d s=4 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s} \) c) \( \dot{Q}=\iint_{S}^{S}\langle 3,4,0\rangle \cdot\left\langle\frac{3}{5}, \frac{4}{3}, 0\right\rangle d S=5 \mathrm{~m}^{3} / \mathrm{s} \) d.) \( \dot{Q}=\iint_{S}\langle 3,4,0\rangle \cdot\left\langle\frac{4}{5},-\frac{3}{5}, 0\right\rangle d S: 0 \) ! 2-19
raw_text: \( \dot{Q}= \) volumetric flow rate of fluid through a surface \( S \). \[ \dot{Q}=\iint_{S} d \dot{Q}=\iint_{S * \dot{Q}=U \cdot A} \vec{U} \cdot \vec{n} d S \quad \begin{array}{l} \forall \cdot L \\ \rightarrow(U, V, W) \text { is uniform and to } A! \end{array} \text { All Scomeon . } \] Example. Velocity field is \( \vec{u}=(3,4,0) \mathrm{m} / \mathrm{s} \). Calculate volumetric flow rates through \( 1 \mathrm{~m}^{2} \) surface \( S \) if a) \( \vec{n}=(1,0,0) \) b) \( \vec{n}=(0,1,0) \) c) \( \vec{n}=\left(\frac{3}{5}, \frac{4}{5}, 0\right) \) d) \( \vec{n}=\left(\frac{4}{5},-\frac{3}{5}, 0\right) \) a.) \( \dot{Q}=\iint_{S}(\vec{u} \cdot \vec{n}) d S=\iint_{S}\langle 3,4,0\rangle \cdot\langle 1,0,0\rangle d S=\iint_{S} 3 d S=3 \iint_{S} d S=3 \mathrm{~m}^{2} / \mathrm{s} \) b.) \( \dot{Q}=\iint\langle 3,4,0\rangle \cdot\langle 0,1,0\rangle d s=4 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s} \) c) \( \dot{Q}=\iint_{S}^{S}\langle 3,4,0\rangle \cdot\left\langle\frac{3}{5}, \frac{4}{3}, 0\right\rangle d S=5 \mathrm{~m}^{3} / \mathrm{s} \) d.) \( \dot{Q}=\iint_{S}\langle 3,4,0\rangle \cdot\left\langle\frac{4}{5},-\frac{3}{5}, 0\right\rangle d S: 0 \) ! 2-19

text: \( \underline{\text { Flux }}= \) rate of flow of a quantity per unit area. For example, volume flow rate per unit area. Flux is a vector in the direction normal to the area \( \vec{n} \). \( \vec{u} \cdot \vec{n}= \) Volumetric flow rate per unit area in direction of \( \vec{u} \cdot \vec{e}_{X}= \) Volumetric flow rate per unit area in direction of \( \vec{u} \cdot \vec{e}_{y}= \) Volumetric flow rate per unit area in direction of \( y \vec{u} \cdot \vec{e}_{Z}= \) Volumetric flow rate per unit area in direction of \( z \) \[ \vec{u}=\langle u, v, w\rangle \text { or }\left\langle u_{x}, u_{y}, u_{z}\right\rangle \] \[ \dot{Q}=\oiint_{S} \underbrace{\vec{u} \cdot \vec{n}}_{\substack{\text { volumetric } \\ \text { fluw }}} d S \] flux \( \vec{u} \rho(\vec{u} \cdot \vec{n}) \) mometem flux! 2-20

Flux is a vector in the direction normal to the area \( \vec{n} \). \( \vec{u} \cdot \vec{n}= \) Volumetric flow rate per unit area in direction of \( \vec{u} \cdot \vec{e}_{X}= \) Volumetric flow rate per unit area in direction of \( \vec{u} \cdot \vec{e}_{y}= \) Volumetric flow rate per unit area in direction of \( y \vec{u} \cdot \vec{e}_{Z}= \) Volumetric flow rate per unit area in direction of \( z \) \[ \vec{u}=\langle u, v, w\rangle \text { or }\left\langle u_{x}, u_{y}, u_{z}\right\rangle \] \[ \dot{Q}=\oiint_{S} \underbrace{\vec{u} \cdot \vec{n}}_{\substack{\text { volumetric } \\ \text { fluw }}} d S \] flux \( \vec{u} \rho(\vec{u} \cdot \vec{n}) \) mometem flux! 2-20

text: Mass flow rate \( d \dot{m}= \) rate of flow of mass through a differential surface \( d S \). \( d \dot{m}=\rho \vec{u} \cdot \vec{n} d S=\rho d \dot{Q} \) if \( \rho \) = constant \[ \dot{m}=\oiint_{S} d \dot{m}=\oiint_{S} \underbrace{\rho \vec{u} \cdot \vec{n}}_{\text {both }} d S \] throus \( 5(-) \) If density is constant ( \( \rho= \) const): \[ \dot{m}=\rho \oiint_{S} \vec{u} \cdot \vec{n} d S: \rho \dot{Q}=\rho \cup A \] 2-21
raw_text: Mass flow rate \( d \dot{m}= \) rate of flow of mass through a differential surface \( d S \). \( d \dot{m}=\rho \vec{u} \cdot \vec{n} d S=\rho d \dot{Q} \) if \( \rho \) = constant \[ \dot{m}=\oiint_{S} d \dot{m}=\oiint_{S} \underbrace{\rho \vec{u} \cdot \vec{n}}_{\text {both }} d S \] throus \( 5(-) \) If density is constant ( \( \rho= \) const): \[ \dot{m}=\rho \oiint_{S} \vec{u} \cdot \vec{n} d S: \rho \dot{Q}=\rho \cup A \] 2-21

text: Conservation of Mass Apply the fundamental principle that mass is not created or destroyed to a fixed control volume. \[ \begin{array}{l} \text { time } \\ {\left[\begin{array}{l} \text { rate of mass } \\ \text { accumulation } \\ \text { in } V \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{l} \text { rate of } \\ \text { mass flow } \\ \text { in } \end{array}\right]-\left[\begin{array}{c} \text { rate of } \\ \text { mass flow } \\ \text { out } \end{array}\right]} \end{array} \] - Globel (Integrel): Use fie C.V. anclysis uf rystems - Local Form: Help eveluete flow at a point Evaluate terms: \[ \left[\begin{array}{l} \text { time } \\ \text { rate of mass } \\ \text { accumulation } \\ \text { in } V \end{array}\right]=\frac{\partial}{\partial t}\left[\begin{array}{l} t^{\text {total }} \\ \text { mass of fluid in } \mathrm{V} \end{array}\right]=\frac{\partial}{\partial t} \underbrace{\oiint_{V} \rho d V} \] Integral of 1 2-22 Mess in \( v \) !
raw_text: Conservation of Mass Apply the fundamental principle that mass is not created or destroyed to a fixed control volume. \[ \begin{array}{l} \text { time } \\ {\left[\begin{array}{l} \text { rate of mass } \\ \text { accumulation } \\ \text { in } V \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{l} \text { rate of } \\ \text { mass flow } \\ \text { in } \end{array}\right]-\left[\begin{array}{c} \text { rate of } \\ \text { mass flow } \\ \text { out } \end{array}\right]} \end{array} \] - Globel (Integrel): Use fie C.V. anclysis uf rystems - Local Form: Help eveluete flow at a point Evaluate terms: \[ \left[\begin{array}{l} \text { time } \\ \text { rate of mass } \\ \text { accumulation } \\ \text { in } V \end{array}\right]=\frac{\partial}{\partial t}\left[\begin{array}{l} t^{\text {total }} \\ \text { mass of fluid in } \mathrm{V} \end{array}\right]=\frac{\partial}{\partial t} \underbrace{\oiint_{V} \rho d V} \] Integral of 1 2-22 Mess in \( v \) !

text: (global conservation of mass) (Integral Form. \( 2 \frac{\partial}{\partial t} \) ort applies If V is fixed \( \Rightarrow \quad \frac{\partial}{\partial t} \oiiint_{V} \rho d V=\oiiint_{V} \frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} d V \quad V_{i} \) is \( f_{i+2} \) *Apply divergence theorem: \[ \oiint_{S} \vec{f} \cdot \vec{n} d S=\oiiint_{V} \nabla \cdot \vec{f} d V, \quad \vec{d}=\rho \vec{u} \quad \Rightarrow \] 2-23
raw_text: (global conservation of mass) (Integral Form. \( 2 \frac{\partial}{\partial t} \) ort applies If V is fixed \( \Rightarrow \quad \frac{\partial}{\partial t} \oiiint_{V} \rho d V=\oiiint_{V} \frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} d V \quad V_{i} \) is \( f_{i+2} \) *Apply divergence theorem: \[ \oiint_{S} \vec{f} \cdot \vec{n} d S=\oiiint_{V} \nabla \cdot \vec{f} d V, \quad \vec{d}=\rho \vec{u} \quad \Rightarrow \] 2-23

text: Substitute this into the global mass conservation for volume V: Volume \( V \) is arbitrary, therefore, the integrand must be zero everywhere \( \Rightarrow \) \[ \frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t}+\nabla \cdot(\rho \vec{u})=0 \quad \begin{array}{c} \text { local mass conservation } \\ \text { (continuity equation) } \end{array} \] - Orly assumption to this part is continum flow! - Good for viscous or inviscid, compressible or incompressible flowl 2-24

text: Quasi 1D approximation useful for nozzle flows (AAE334) Assume: (a) fluid properties (density, velocity, pressure) depend only on \( x \). (b) flow is steady. \( \frac{\partial()}{\partial t}=0 \) 2-26

text: Apply global mass conservation. \[ \frac{\partial}{\partial t} \int_{V} p d V=-\oint_{s}^{0} p \vec{u} \cdot \vec{n} d s \] \[ \begin{array}{l} \text { Flow is steady } \\ \iint_{S_{1}} \rho_{1} \vec{u}_{i} \cdot \vec{n}_{1} d S+\iint_{S_{2}} \rho_{2} \vec{u}_{2} \cdot \vec{n}_{2} d S=0 \\ p_{1}(u, \hat{\imath}) \cdot(-\hat{\imath})\left(A_{1}\right)+p_{2}\left(u_{2} \hat{\imath}\right) \cdot(\Delta \hat{\imath}) A_{2}=0 \\ \begin{array}{c} \uparrow \\ \text { unform } u_{1}: \\ \text { voer arca: } \end{array} \quad-p_{1} u_{1} A_{1}+p_{2} u_{2} A_{2}=0 \\ \rho_{1} u_{1} A_{1}=\rho_{2} u_{2} A_{2} \\ \rho \text { и } A=\text { const }=\dot{m} \end{array} \] If the fluid is incompressible ( \( \rho= \) constant) \( \Rightarrow \quad U \cdot A= \) constat \( =\dot{Q} \) (Volumetric Flow Rate) Average velocity: (Good for mess flow problems typicoly not youd) \[ \vec{u}=\frac{\dot{Q}}{A}=\frac{1}{A} \iint_{S} \vec{u} \cdot \vec{n} d S \] Through ore: 2-27 swface.
raw_text: Apply global mass conservation. \[ \frac{\partial}{\partial t} \int_{V} p d V=-\oint_{s}^{0} p \vec{u} \cdot \vec{n} d s \] \[ \begin{array}{l} \text { Flow is steady } \\ \iint_{S_{1}} \rho_{1} \vec{u}_{i} \cdot \vec{n}_{1} d S+\iint_{S_{2}} \rho_{2} \vec{u}_{2} \cdot \vec{n}_{2} d S=0 \\ p_{1}(u, \hat{\imath}) \cdot(-\hat{\imath})\left(A_{1}\right)+p_{2}\left(u_{2} \hat{\imath}\right) \cdot(\Delta \hat{\imath}) A_{2}=0 \\ \begin{array}{c} \uparrow \\ \text { unform } u_{1}: \\ \text { voer arca: } \end{array} \quad-p_{1} u_{1} A_{1}+p_{2} u_{2} A_{2}=0 \\ \rho_{1} u_{1} A_{1}=\rho_{2} u_{2} A_{2} \\ \rho \text { и } A=\text { const }=\dot{m} \end{array} \] If the fluid is incompressible ( \( \rho= \) constant) \( \Rightarrow \quad U \cdot A= \) constat \( =\dot{Q} \) (Volumetric Flow Rate) Average velocity: (Good for mess flow problems typicoly not youd) \[ \vec{u}=\frac{\dot{Q}}{A}=\frac{1}{A} \iint_{S} \vec{u} \cdot \vec{n} d S \] Through ore: 2-27 swface.

text: Example: Laminar flow in a circular pipe. Find averse velocity! Parabolic velocity profile: \[ \begin{array}{l} u_{z}(r)=u_{c}\left(1-\frac{r^{2}}{R^{2}}\right) \\ \left.\bar{U}_{z}=\frac{\dot{Q}}{A}=\frac{1}{A} \iint_{S}(\vec{u} \cdot \vec{n}) d S\right\} \begin{array}{l} \vec{u}=\vec{U}_{z}(r) \hat{e}_{z} \\ \vec{n}=\hat{e}_{z} \end{array} \rightarrow \frac{1}{\pi R^{2}} \int_{0}^{R} \int_{0}^{2 \pi} U_{z}(r) \underbrace{r \theta d r}_{d S} \end{array} \] \[ \begin{array}{l} =2 u_{c} \int_{0}^{1}\left(1-s^{2}\right) s d s=2 u_{c}\left[\frac{s^{2}}{2}-\frac{s^{4}}{4}\right]_{0}^{1}=2 u_{c}\left(\frac{1}{2}-\frac{1}{4}\right)= \\ \Rightarrow \bar{u}_{z}=\frac{u_{c}}{2} \end{array} \]
raw_text: Example: Laminar flow in a circular pipe. Find averse velocity! Parabolic velocity profile: \[ \begin{array}{l} u_{z}(r)=u_{c}\left(1-\frac{r^{2}}{R^{2}}\right) \\ \left.\bar{U}_{z}=\frac{\dot{Q}}{A}=\frac{1}{A} \iint_{S}(\vec{u} \cdot \vec{n}) d S\right\} \begin{array}{l} \vec{u}=\vec{U}_{z}(r) \hat{e}_{z} \\ \vec{n}=\hat{e}_{z} \end{array} \rightarrow \frac{1}{\pi R^{2}} \int_{0}^{R} \int_{0}^{2 \pi} U_{z}(r) \underbrace{r \theta d r}_{d S} \end{array} \] \[ \begin{array}{l} =2 u_{c} \int_{0}^{1}\left(1-s^{2}\right) s d s=2 u_{c}\left[\frac{s^{2}}{2}-\frac{s^{4}}{4}\right]_{0}^{1}=2 u_{c}\left(\frac{1}{2}-\frac{1}{4}\right)= \\ \Rightarrow \bar{u}_{z}=\frac{u_{c}}{2} \end{array} \]

text: Overview of Vector Calculus Vector is a quantity, completely specified by a magnitude and a direction. \( \hat{\imath}, \hat{\jmath}, \hat{\kappa} \) \( \vec{\iota} \vec{j} \vec{h}= \) Cartesian basis vectors \[ \vec{u}=u_{1} \vec{i}+u_{2} \vec{j}+u_{3} \vec{k} \] Vector can be denoted by one-dimensional array of its coordinates: \( \left(u_{1}, u_{2}, u_{3}\right) \) Magnitude \( |\vec{u}|=\sqrt{u_{1}^{2}+u_{2}^{2}+u_{3}^{2}} \) 2-2
raw_text: Overview of Vector Calculus Vector is a quantity, completely specified by a magnitude and a direction. \( \hat{\imath}, \hat{\jmath}, \hat{\kappa} \) \( \vec{\iota} \vec{j} \vec{h}= \) Cartesian basis vectors \[ \vec{u}=u_{1} \vec{i}+u_{2} \vec{j}+u_{3} \vec{k} \] Vector can be denoted by one-dimensional array of its coordinates: \( \left(u_{1}, u_{2}, u_{3}\right) \) Magnitude \( |\vec{u}|=\sqrt{u_{1}^{2}+u_{2}^{2}+u_{3}^{2}} \) 2-2

text: Example: Given inlet velocity and radius and pipe radius. Find centerline velocity. in terms of \( u_{\infty} \) \[ \begin{array}{l} -\iint_{S_{1}} \rho \vec{u}_{1} \cdot \vec{n}_{1} d S_{1}-\iint_{S_{2}} \rho \vec{u}_{2} \cdot \vec{n}_{2} d S_{2}=0 \quad \vec{u}_{1}=v_{\infty} \hat{e}_{z} \\ S_{2} \text { Assum incompressible so } \rho=\rho=\rho \\ \text { At S. } \\ \vec{u}_{1}=U_{\infty} \hat{e}_{z} \\ \text { At (S2) } \\ \vec{n}_{1}=-\hat{e}_{z} \\ \vec{u}_{2}=U_{c}\left(1-\frac{r^{2}}{R^{2}}\right) \hat{e}_{z} \\ \text { or } \bar{u}_{z}=\frac{U_{c}}{2} \hat{e}_{z} \\ -\int_{0}^{2 \pi} \int_{0}^{R} p\left(u_{\infty} \hat{e}_{z}\right) \cdot\left(-\hat{e}_{z}\right) r d r d \theta-\hat{e}_{0} \int_{0}^{2 \pi} R p\left(\frac{u_{c}}{2} \hat{e}_{z}\right) \cdot\left(\hat{e}_{z}\right) r d r d \theta=0 \\ \int_{0}^{0} \int_{0}^{2 \pi} \int_{0}^{R} u_{\infty} r d r d \theta-\int_{0}^{2 \pi} \int_{0}^{R} \frac{u_{c}}{2} r d r d \theta=0 \end{array} \]
raw_text: Example: Given inlet velocity and radius and pipe radius. Find centerline velocity. in terms of \( u_{\infty} \) \[ \begin{array}{l} -\iint_{S_{1}} \rho \vec{u}_{1} \cdot \vec{n}_{1} d S_{1}-\iint_{S_{2}} \rho \vec{u}_{2} \cdot \vec{n}_{2} d S_{2}=0 \quad \vec{u}_{1}=v_{\infty} \hat{e}_{z} \\ S_{2} \text { Assum incompressible so } \rho=\rho=\rho \\ \text { At S. } \\ \vec{u}_{1}=U_{\infty} \hat{e}_{z} \\ \text { At (S2) } \\ \vec{n}_{1}=-\hat{e}_{z} \\ \vec{u}_{2}=U_{c}\left(1-\frac{r^{2}}{R^{2}}\right) \hat{e}_{z} \\ \text { or } \bar{u}_{z}=\frac{U_{c}}{2} \hat{e}_{z} \\ -\int_{0}^{2 \pi} \int_{0}^{R} p\left(u_{\infty} \hat{e}_{z}\right) \cdot\left(-\hat{e}_{z}\right) r d r d \theta-\hat{e}_{0} \int_{0}^{2 \pi} R p\left(\frac{u_{c}}{2} \hat{e}_{z}\right) \cdot\left(\hat{e}_{z}\right) r d r d \theta=0 \\ \int_{0}^{0} \int_{0}^{2 \pi} \int_{0}^{R} u_{\infty} r d r d \theta-\int_{0}^{2 \pi} \int_{0}^{R} \frac{u_{c}}{2} r d r d \theta=0 \end{array} \]

text: Example: Water enters a cylindrical tank through two pipes (Surfaces 1 and 2) and exits through a single pipe (Surface 3). If the level in the tank remains constant, calculate the velocity of the flow leaving the tank. Assume constant density and uniform velocity for each inlet / outlet. \[ \frac{\partial()}{\partial t}=0 \text { (steady flow) } \] 2-31

If the level in the tank remains constant, calculate the velocity of the flow leaving the tank. Assume constant density and uniform velocity for each inlet / outlet. \[ \frac{\partial()}{\partial t}=0 \text { (steady flow) } \] 2-31

text: \[ \frac{\partial}{\partial t} \oiint_{V} \rho d V=-\oiint_{S} \rho \vec{u} \cdot \vec{n} d S \] 0 (steady) \[ O=-\int_{S} \rho \vec{u} \cdot \vec{n} d S \] \( \alpha V_{1} A_{1}+\rho V_{2} A_{2}-\rho A_{3} V_{3}=0 \) \[ V_{3}=\frac{\left(V_{1} A_{1}+V_{2} A_{2}\right)}{A_{3}} \] * Flow is uniform across ecch surface and ingumpressible so we can use our shritent \( m=\rho U A \) 2-32

text: Water enters a cylindrical tank through two pipes (Surfaces 1 and 2) and exits through a single pipe (Surface 3). If the level in the tank DOES NOT remain constant, calculate the time rate change of the level in the tank. Assume constant density and uniform velocity for each inlet / outlet. 2-33

text: \[ \frac{\partial}{\partial t} \int_{V} p d V=-\int_{S} p \vec{u} \cdot \vec{n} d S \] Since peoonstent, \( \chi \frac{\partial}{\partial t} \int_{V} d V=-\gamma \int_{S} \vec{s} \cdot \vec{n} d S \) \[ \begin{array}{l} \frac{\partial}{\partial t}\left(\pi R^{2} h\right)=V_{1} A_{1}+V_{2} A_{2}-V_{3} A_{3} \\ \frac{d h}{d t}=\frac{V_{1} A_{1}+V_{2} A_{2}-V_{3} A_{3}}{\pi R^{2}} \end{array} \] 2-34
raw_text: \[ \frac{\partial}{\partial t} \int_{V} p d V=-\int_{S} p \vec{u} \cdot \vec{n} d S \] Since peoonstent, \( \chi \frac{\partial}{\partial t} \int_{V} d V=-\gamma \int_{S} \vec{s} \cdot \vec{n} d S \) \[ \begin{array}{l} \frac{\partial}{\partial t}\left(\pi R^{2} h\right)=V_{1} A_{1}+V_{2} A_{2}-V_{3} A_{3} \\ \frac{d h}{d t}=\frac{V_{1} A_{1}+V_{2} A_{2}-V_{3} A_{3}}{\pi R^{2}} \end{array} \] 2-34

text: A small hole is punched in the side of a jug of vinegar. If the velocity at the exit is \( \mathrm{v}=(2 \mathrm{gh})^{1 / 2} \), determine the following: a.) The height of the vinegar versus time. Not b.) The time it takes to completely drain. stead End alleles 2-35

text: Vector addition \( \vec{A}=\left(a_{1}, a_{2}, a_{3}\right), \vec{B}=\left(b_{1}, b_{2}, b_{3}\right) \) \[ \vec{C}=\vec{A}+\vec{B}=\left(a_{1}+b_{1}, a_{2}+b_{2}, a_{3}+b_{3}\right) \] Scalar is a quantity, such as presure (P), Temperature, etc. that is completely specified by its magnitude and has no direction. Product of a scalar and a vector Inner (dot) product of two vectors \[ \vec{A} \cdot \vec{B}=|\vec{A}||\vec{B}| \cos \theta=a_{1} b_{1}+a_{2} b_{2}+a_{3} b_{3} \] *Two vectors are orthogonal if \( \vec{A} \cdot \vec{B}=0 \) \[ \underset{\substack{\uparrow \\ \text { sclukr }}}{\alpha \vec{A}}=\underbrace{\left(\alpha a_{1}, \alpha a_{2}, \alpha a_{3}\right)}_{\text {vector }} \] Vector (cross) product of two vectors \[ \begin{array}{l} \qquad \begin{array}{c} \vec{A} \times \vec{B}=(\vec{A}| | \vec{B} \mid \sin \theta) \vec{e}=\left|\begin{array}{ccc} \vec{i} & \vec{j} & \vec{k} \\ a_{1} & a_{2} & a_{3} \\ b_{1} & b_{2} & b_{3} \end{array}\right| \\ =\left(a_{2} b_{3}-a_{3} b_{2}\right) \hat{\imath}-\left(a_{1} b_{3}-a_{3} b_{1}\right) \hat{\jmath} \hat{\jmath} \\ +\left(a_{1} b_{2}-a_{2} b_{1}\right) \hat{k} \end{array} \end{array} \] 2-3
raw_text: Vector addition \( \vec{A}=\left(a_{1}, a_{2}, a_{3}\right), \vec{B}=\left(b_{1}, b_{2}, b_{3}\right) \) \[ \vec{C}=\vec{A}+\vec{B}=\left(a_{1}+b_{1}, a_{2}+b_{2}, a_{3}+b_{3}\right) \] Scalar is a quantity, such as presure (P), Temperature, etc. that is completely specified by its magnitude and has no direction. Product of a scalar and a vector Inner (dot) product of two vectors \[ \vec{A} \cdot \vec{B}=|\vec{A}||\vec{B}| \cos \theta=a_{1} b_{1}+a_{2} b_{2}+a_{3} b_{3} \] *Two vectors are orthogonal if \( \vec{A} \cdot \vec{B}=0 \) \[ \underset{\substack{\uparrow \\ \text { sclukr }}}{\alpha \vec{A}}=\underbrace{\left(\alpha a_{1}, \alpha a_{2}, \alpha a_{3}\right)}_{\text {vector }} \] Vector (cross) product of two vectors \[ \begin{array}{l} \qquad \begin{array}{c} \vec{A} \times \vec{B}=(\vec{A}| | \vec{B} \mid \sin \theta) \vec{e}=\left|\begin{array}{ccc} \vec{i} & \vec{j} & \vec{k} \\ a_{1} & a_{2} & a_{3} \\ b_{1} & b_{2} & b_{3} \end{array}\right| \\ =\left(a_{2} b_{3}-a_{3} b_{2}\right) \hat{\imath}-\left(a_{1} b_{3}-a_{3} b_{1}\right) \hat{\jmath} \hat{\jmath} \\ +\left(a_{1} b_{2}-a_{2} b_{1}\right) \hat{k} \end{array} \end{array} \] 2-3

text: Cartesian system of coordinates is an example of orthogonal coordinate system. Other orthogonal coordinate systems: cylindrical and spherical. Cylindrical coordinate system: \[ \begin{array}{l} r=\sqrt{x^{2}+y^{2}} \\ \theta=\arctan \left(\frac{y}{x}\right) \\ z=z \end{array} \] 2-4
raw_text: Cartesian system of coordinates is an example of orthogonal coordinate system. Other orthogonal coordinate systems: cylindrical and spherical. Cylindrical coordinate system: \[ \begin{array}{l} r=\sqrt{x^{2}+y^{2}} \\ \theta=\arctan \left(\frac{y}{x}\right) \\ z=z \end{array} \] 2-4

text: Spherical coordinate system \[ \begin{aligned} r & =\sqrt{x^{2}+y^{2}+z^{2}} \\ \theta & =\arccos \left(\frac{z}{x^{2}+y^{2}+z^{2}}\right) \\ \phi & =\arctan \left(\frac{y}{x}\right) \end{aligned} \] \[ \left.\begin{array}{l} \vec{e}_{r}=\sin \theta \cos \phi \vec{i}+\sin \theta \sin \phi \vec{j}+\cos \theta \vec{k} \\ \vec{e}_{\theta}=\cos \theta \cos \phi \vec{i}+\cos \theta \sin \phi \vec{j}-\sin \theta \vec{k} \\ \vec{e}_{\phi}=-\sin \phi \vec{i}+\cos \phi \vec{j} \end{array}\right\} \text { Vectors } \] 2-5
raw_text: Spherical coordinate system \[ \begin{aligned} r & =\sqrt{x^{2}+y^{2}+z^{2}} \\ \theta & =\arccos \left(\frac{z}{x^{2}+y^{2}+z^{2}}\right) \\ \phi & =\arctan \left(\frac{y}{x}\right) \end{aligned} \] \[ \left.\begin{array}{l} \vec{e}_{r}=\sin \theta \cos \phi \vec{i}+\sin \theta \sin \phi \vec{j}+\cos \theta \vec{k} \\ \vec{e}_{\theta}=\cos \theta \cos \phi \vec{i}+\cos \theta \sin \phi \vec{j}-\sin \theta \vec{k} \\ \vec{e}_{\phi}=-\sin \phi \vec{i}+\cos \phi \vec{j} \end{array}\right\} \text { Vectors } \] 2-5

text: Scalar Field is a scalar quantity given as a function of coordinate space and time \( p(x, y, z, t) \) or \( T(x, y, z, t) \) Vector Field is a vector quantity given as a function of coordinate space and time. Gradient of a scalar field \( \vec{v}(x, y, z, t) \) Let \( p \) be a scalar field The gradient of \( \mathrm{p}, 7 \mathrm{p} \), at a given point in space is defined as a vector such that: - Its magnitude is equal to the maximum rate of change of \( p \) per unit length of the coordinate space at the given point - Its direction is that of the maximum rate of change of \( p \) at the given point. In Cartesian coordinates: \( \quad \nabla p=\frac{\partial p}{\partial x} \vec{i}+\frac{\partial p}{\partial y} \vec{j}+\frac{\partial p}{\partial z} \vec{k} \) 2-6 \[ \nabla=\left\langle\frac{\partial}{\partial x}, \frac{\partial}{\partial y}, \frac{\partial}{\partial z}\right\rangle \]
raw_text: Scalar Field is a scalar quantity given as a function of coordinate space and time \( p(x, y, z, t) \) or \( T(x, y, z, t) \) Vector Field is a vector quantity given as a function of coordinate space and time. Gradient of a scalar field \( \vec{v}(x, y, z, t) \) Let \( p \) be a scalar field The gradient of \( \mathrm{p}, 7 \mathrm{p} \), at a given point in space is defined as a vector such that: - Its magnitude is equal to the maximum rate of change of \( p \) per unit length of the coordinate space at the given point - Its direction is that of the maximum rate of change of \( p \) at the given point. In Cartesian coordinates: \( \quad \nabla p=\frac{\partial p}{\partial x} \vec{i}+\frac{\partial p}{\partial y} \vec{j}+\frac{\partial p}{\partial z} \vec{k} \) 2-6 \[ \nabla=\left\langle\frac{\partial}{\partial x}, \frac{\partial}{\partial y}, \frac{\partial}{\partial z}\right\rangle \]

text: Line, Surface and Volume Integrals Line Integrals (also called Path Integrals) (a) Line integral of a scalar field \( A \) along a curve \( C \) is written as \[ \int_{C} A d s . \quad \text { For example, if } \rho_{t} \text { is the linear }{ }_{t} \text { density of a wire, } \] then the line integral is its mass. \( \int_{c} p d s=\int_{0}^{L} p d s=p l= \) mass! (b) Line integral of a vector field \( \vec{A} \) along a curve C is written as The line integral can be thought of as a measure of the total effect of a given vector field along a given curve. For example, the work done on a particle traveling on a curve \( C \) in a force field \( \vec{F} \) is equal to line integral of \( \vec{F} \) along \( C \). Think work due to gravity!!! 2-9
raw_text: Line, Surface and Volume Integrals Line Integrals (also called Path Integrals) (a) Line integral of a scalar field \( A \) along a curve \( C \) is written as \[ \int_{C} A d s . \quad \text { For example, if } \rho_{t} \text { is the linear }{ }_{t} \text { density of a wire, } \] then the line integral is its mass. \( \int_{c} p d s=\int_{0}^{L} p d s=p l= \) mass! (b) Line integral of a vector field \( \vec{A} \) along a curve C is written as The line integral can be thought of as a measure of the total effect of a given vector field along a given curve. For example, the work done on a particle traveling on a curve \( C \) in a force field \( \vec{F} \) is equal to line integral of \( \vec{F} \) along \( C \). Think work due to gravity!!! 2-9
